one_line_takeaway: Diffusion-based image regeneration (unguided) and a proposed decoder-guided variant drive decoding accuracy of StegaStamp, TrustMark, and VINE-R robust watermarks down to 7.4-24.5% and 0.0-1.6% respectively while preserving high visual fidelity (PSNR ~30-32 dB, SSIM 0.93-0.95), and information-theoretic analysis argues this watermark erasure is a fundamental, not incidental, property of generative image regeneration.
key_finding: Table 1 (500 COCO images, averaged): baseline (no attack) decoding is 99.8% (StegaStamp), 99.9% (TrustMark), 100.0% (VINE-R). Standard distortions leave decoding largely intact: e.g. StegaStamp 92.5% under JPEG-50, 88.1% under 10% crop, 90.3% under noise (sigma=10); TrustMark 94.7%/91.2%/95.5%; VINE-R 96.4%/93.0%/97.8% for the same three distortions respectively. The unguided diffusion regeneration attack collapses decoding to 7.4% (StegaStamp), 12.8% (TrustMark), and 24.5% (VINE-R). The guided removal attack drives decoding to 0.0% for StegaStamp and TrustMark and to 1.6% for VINE-R. Image fidelity of the attacked outputs remains high: regeneration attack yields PSNR 31.8 dB, SSIM 0.95, LPIPS 0.03 vs. the watermarked original; the guided attack trades slightly more fidelity for completeness (PSNR 30.5 dB, SSIM 0.93, LPIPS 0.05). A dose-response experiment varying diffusion noise strength t shows StegaStamp decoding already below 50% by t=0.2 and near-zero by t=0.3-0.4, identifying a 'sweet spot' where visual content is preserved but the watermark is gone. A secondary GAN (StyleGAN projection/reconstruction) experiment on a face image dropped StegaStamp decoding from 100% to 20%, suggesting the vulnerability is not diffusion-exclusive but that diffusion models are a more potent/faithful eraser due to iterative refinement.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is threefold: (1) an empirical demonstration that three state-of-the-art robust invisible watermarking schemes (StegaStamp, TrustMark, VINE-R) suffer near-total decoding failure when a watermarked image is passed through a diffusion-based image-to-image regeneration process, far beyond the degradation caused by conventional distortions (JPEG, crop, noise); (2) a novel guided diffusion watermark-removal attack (Algorithm 1) that backpropagates a watermark-decoder loss (||decoder(x0) - target_null_message||^2) through the final denoising step(s) of the diffusion sampling process to explicitly drive the decoded payload toward a null/dummy message; and (3) a theoretical argument (Theorem 1, proof sketch via a Markov chain W->X->N->Y and the data-processing inequality) that mutual information between the watermark payload W and the diffusion-edited image Y tends to zero as diffusion noise/length increases, because the watermark's high-frequency, non-semantic bit pattern is destroyed in the forward noising process and cannot be reconstructed by the reverse generative process.
threat_model: Adversary has access to a diffusion model (Stable Diffusion v1.5 used in experiments) and a watermarked image. Two adversary capability levels are considered: (a) unguided/benign-or-malicious use -- attacker (or an unaware end user) simply runs image-to-image regeneration or inpainting with low denoising strength, no knowledge of the watermark scheme needed; (b) guided/white-box removal -- attacker additionally has access to the watermark decoder's output/gradients (though not necessarily its secret key) and uses decoder feedback within the diffusion denoising loop to actively steer the output toward a null message. Goal in both cases is to preserve visual/semantic content while destroying the embedded payload, applicable to copyright/provenance/content-authenticity watermarking (e.g., C2PA-style schemes) and to deepfake-detection watermark evasion.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: only one base diffusion model (SD1.5) was tested for the main experiments, and other model families (SDXL, DiT/FLUX, text-to-video diffusion) were not evaluated for the attack, though the paper speculates similar effects would hold; no video watermarks were tested; the guided attack applies only a single gradient step at the final denoising iteration rather than multi-step optimization across all steps, so a more sophisticated attacker could plausibly do better; the guided attack assumes decoder access, and the paper only speculates (does not test) that a fully blind attacker without decoder access would still succeed via the unguided path; the theoretical Theorem 1 is explicitly described as an 'idealized statement' with an informal proof sketch (not a rigorous formal proof) that assumes an ideal/perfect diffusion regeneration process and does not tightly bound finite-step, finite-capacity real models; traditional (non-deep) spread-spectrum watermarks were not empirically tested, only discussed qualitatively; no proactive detector for 'this image was watermarked and then laundered' was found to work, left as an open problem.

quantitative_results: StegaStamp decoding: 99.8% (no attack) -> 92.5% (JPEG-50) -> 88.1% (10% crop) -> 90.3% (noise sigma=10) -> 7.4% (unguided diffusion regeneration) -> 0.0% (guided diffusion removal)
TrustMark decoding: 99.9% (no attack) -> 94.7% (JPEG-50) -> 91.2% (10% crop) -> 95.5% (noise sigma=10) -> 12.8% (unguided regeneration) -> 0.0% (guided removal)
VINE-R decoding: 100.0% (no attack) -> 96.4% (JPEG-50) -> 93.0% (10% crop) -> 97.8% (noise sigma=10) -> 24.5% (unguided regeneration) -> 1.6% (guided removal)
Regeneration attack image fidelity vs. watermarked original: PSNR 31.8 dB, SSIM 0.95, LPIPS 0.03
Guided removal attack image fidelity: PSNR 30.5 dB, SSIM 0.93, LPIPS 0.05
StegaStamp decoding falls below 50% by diffusion noise level t=0.2 and near-zero by t=0.3-0.4
StyleGAN latent projection/reconstruction (single face image test) dropped StegaStamp decoding from 100% to 20%
baselines_compared: Additive Gaussian noise (sigma=10, tuned to severely degrade)
GAN-based (StyleGAN) latent reconstruction, as an alternative generative-model comparison
